entities.name: Beelzebub
entities.description: The lord of the flies; an idol or oracle at Ekron; reputed as the principal devil among the Jews.

entities.name: Astaroth
entities.description: An idol of the Philistines, also worshipped by Solomon; signifies riches and flocks.

entities.name: Sathan (Satan)
entities.description: The adversary; can hurt both by day and night; described as the author of sin.

entities.name: Mammon
entities.description: Represents the covetous desire for money.

entities.name: Baaros
entities.description: A root mentioned as being one with Solomon's root, having force to drive out devils.

entities.name: James Sprenger
entities.description: A writer who gathers and notes causes for why the devil possesses men at certain moon phases.

entities.name: Bodin (Jean Bodin)
entities.description: A writer who asserts there are day and night devils, and that the devil loves no salt in his meat.

entities.name: Salomon (Solomon)
entities.description: A historical figure associated with conjuration and the worship of certain idols.

entities.name: Deber
entities.description: A devil that hurts by night.

entities.name: Cheleb
entities.description: A devil that hurts by day.

entities.name: Penates
entities.description: Domestic gods or devils (Minerva) said to make men live quietly indoors.

entities.name: Lares
entities.description: Spirits that oversee crossroads and cities; trouble private houses.

summary: This passage from Reginald Scot's 'Discoverie of Witchcraft' explores the nature, names, and origins of various spirits, devils, and pagan gods. Scot provides a linguistic and historical analysis of the names of biblical and classical deities, arguing that many are actually demons or idols used to deceive humanity.
